signals: Treatment is supportive and directed toward hydration and pain relief
Lesions usually resolve in seven to 10 days

extraction.summary: Hand-foot-and-mouth disease is a common viral illness caused by enteroviruses and coxsackieviruses, presenting with low-grade fever, characteristic maculopapular or papulovesicular rash on hands and soles of feet, and painful oral ulcerations. While usually self-limiting, rare neurologic or cardiopulmonary complications can occur, especially with enterovirus 71.

population.age_criteria: Most cases occur in patients younger than 10 years, with largest incidence within first 5 years of life
population.risk_criteria: Children in childcare settings, healthcare workers caring for children, household contacts of infected individuals
population.disease_definition: Clinical diagnosis based on fever with maculopapular or papulovesicular rash on hands and soles of feet plus painful oral ulcerations
population.severity_staging: Uncomplicated disease with low fatality rate (0.06%-0.11%) in US, but severe complications possible with enterovirus 71

key_thresholds.context: Duration of potential transmission via fecal route
Time from exposure to symptom onset

recommendations.recommendation: Weight-based acetaminophen or ibuprofen for fever and pain

recommendations.notes: Lack of benefit and potential harm in children
Especially after diaper changes, toileting, and before eating

recommendations.recommendation: Supportive care with focus on maintaining adequate oral intake
Topical lidocaine for painful oral lesions to improve oral intake
recommendations.notes: Blinded, randomized, placebo-controlled trial showed benefit

medications.indication: Pain relief for oral ulcers to improve oral intake
Limited evidence for use in hand-foot-and-mouth disease

red_flags: Neurologic complications (aseptic meningitis, acute flaccid paralysis, encephalomyelitis)
Cardiopulmonary complications (pulmonary edema, pulmonary hemorrhage, cardiorespiratory failure)
Inability to tolerate oral intake due to severe oral pain
Seizures secondary to lidocaine toxicity if using oral viscous lidocaine
Signs of dehydration from poor oral intake
extraction.follow_up: Monitor for adequate hydration and resolution of symptoms within 7-10 days. Watch for rare complications including delayed nail separation or horizontal nail ridges.
extraction.escalation_path: Hospitalize for failure to maintain adequate hydration or development of neurologic or cardiopulmonary complications. Consider specialized consultation for severe cases or atypical presentations.
